examples.title: Creating and Executing a Display List
examples.description: Demonstrates basic display list creation and usage in OpenGL-like syntax
examples.explanation: This code shows how to create a display list that stores graphics commands (defining a red square) and then execute it using glCallList(). The commands are compiled and stored in the display list, then rendered when called.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for creation, O(1) for execution
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of primitives

examples.title: Hierarchical Display List Example
examples.description: Demonstrates hierarchical display list with transformations
examples.explanation: This demonstrates hierarchical display lists where transformations cascade. Each arm segment is defined in its own display list, and when rendered, the transformations are applied hierarchically, so rotating arm1 moves the entire arm2 and gripper.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of segments

examples.title: Display List for Repeated Object Rendering
examples.description: Shows efficiency of display lists for objects rendered multiple times
examples.explanation: This shows the efficiency gain - instead of re-specifying all tree geometry 100 times, we create the tree once in a display list and call it repeatedly. Each glCallList is much faster than re-executing all the primitive commands.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per tree after initial compilation

examples.title: Display List with State Changes
examples.description: Demonstrates storing state-changing commands in display lists
examples.explanation: This demonstrates that display lists store not just geometry but also state-changing commands like glColor3f. When executed, the display list's stored state (red color) overrides the current state, ensuring consistent appearance regardless of preceding state.
